title: Deficit Control and Productivity Improvement in the Administration of the Federal Government

flags.justification: The OMB Director defines 'administrative expenses' for all agencies and resolves 'all questions' about that definition (Sec.1), and is separately authorized to unilaterally reduce a noncompliant agency's administrative-expense budget request to force compliance with the escalating 3%/6%/9%/14% (FY94-FY97) reduction schedule (Sec.3).
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

title: Termination and Limitation of Federal Advisory Committees

flags.justification: Requires each agency to terminate at least one-third of its non-statutory FACA committees by the end of FY1993, and bars creation of any new non-statutory advisory committee unless the OMB Director grants approval, which 'shall be granted only sparingly and only if compelled by considerations of national security, health or safety, or similar national interests' (Sec.3) - centralizing government-wide advisory-committee creation authority in OMB.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

title: Additional Measures With Respect to the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia (Serbia and Montenegro)

flags.justification: Escalates beyond blocking Yugoslav *government* property (EO12808/EO12810/EO12831, this stream, Batch 8) to block 'all property and interests in property of all commercial, industrial, or public utility undertakings or entities organized or located in' Serbia/Montenegro (Sec.1(a)) - extending the embargo to the entire formal private and public economy, not merely state assets, implementing UN Security Council Resolution 820.

Blocks all property of commercial, industrial, and public-utility entities located in Serbia/Montenegro (not just government property), authorizes detention and blocking of suspect vessels/cargo/aircraft violating the underlying UN resolutions, bars U.S.-registered vessels from Yugoslav territorial waters, and prohibits dealings involving UN Protected Areas under Bosnian Serb control, implementing UN Security Council Resolution 820 and continuing the emergency from EO12808/EO12810/EO12831 (all this stream, Batch 8).

notes: The most comprehensive order yet in the Yugoslavia sanctions chain - extending blocking from government property alone to the entire commercial/industrial/utility sector - continuing directly from Batch 8's EO12831 (this stream, 33.33%) under the new Clinton administration, confirming the cross-administration continuity predicted at Batch 8's close.

title: Conditions for Renewal of Most-Favored-Nation Status for the Peoples Republic of China in 1994

flags.justification: Conditions the Secretary of State's recommendation on China's Most-Favored-Nation trade status renewal for the 1994-95 period on two binding preconditions (freedom-of-emigration progress; compliance with the 1992 prison-labor agreement) and five non-binding 'overall, significant progress' human-rights factors (Universal Declaration adherence, Tiananmen/Democracy Wall detainee accounting, humane prison treatment, Tibetan cultural protection, international broadcast access), with a recommendation due to the President before June 3, 1994.

title: Federal Compliance With Right-to-Know Laws and Pollution Prevention Requirements

flags.justification: Requires all covered federal facilities to comply with Emergency Planning and Community Right-to-Know Act (EPCRA) Toxics Release Inventory public reporting - previously largely a private-sector-only obligation - and Pollution Prevention Act source-reduction requirements, sets a voluntary 50-percent toxic-chemical-release reduction goal by December 31, 1999, requires agency-specific written pollution-prevention strategies and facility plans, establishes an EPA-led Interagency Task Force and a public recognition program (the Federal Government Environmental Challenge Program), and permits CERCLA-procedure-anchored national-security exemptions.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Core reduction obligations are qualified by 'to the maximum extent practicable' throughout, and Sec.6-601's national-security exemption route, while procedurally anchored to CERCLA section 120(j)(1) procedures, still leaves the underlying compliance obligations largely self-assessed by each agency.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

Sec.4-402's annual-progress-report requirement 'shall expire after the report due on October 1, 2001' - a genuine, dated sunset for the reporting sub-requirement, even though the underlying pollution-prevention and Toxics Release Inventory obligations themselves are not separately sunset.

Sec.6-601's national-security exemption request must follow the established CERCLA section 120(j)(1) statutory procedure rather than a self-created, order-specific channel, distinguishing it from this stream's more common self-determined exemption findings.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

title: Amendments to the Manual for Courts-Martial 1984

flags.justification: Amends the Manual for Courts-Martial to formally implement Article 6a military-judge professional-discipline procedures (with a 'clear and convincing evidence' standard but a final, non-appealable Judge Advocate General decision), revise pretrial-confinement review timelines, update several evidence and immunity rules to track recent federal case law (Taylor v. Illinois, New York v. Harris), and create a new Article 134 offense for wrongfully interfering with adverse administrative proceedings, effective January 21, 1994 with detailed non-retroactivity savings clauses.

Directs all covered federal agencies to make environmental justice part of their mission, establishes a 17-agency Interagency Working Group on Environmental Justice, requires each agency to develop and report a phased environmental-justice strategy over a 24-month timeline, mandates demographic data collection tied to environmental/health effects, addresses subsistence fish/wildlife consumption risks, and creates a public-participation and comment regime, with an open-ended presidential exemption-petition mechanism (Sec.6-605) paralleling EO12892's (this chunk, 25.0%).
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

One of the most consequential and durable orders in this entire corpus: establishes the foundational federal 'environmental justice' policy framework, cited continuously in EPA/DOJ/HUD practice for three decades, formally targeted for elimination early in a subsequent Republican administration and subsequently reaffirmed and expanded by a later Democratic administration (EO14096, 2023) -- a textbook example of a foundational policy order becoming a recurring partisan flashpoint across administrations.
Not applicable to this order's structure or subject matter.

notes: One of this stream's most consequential findings to date: a foundational, still-contested policy order (CRITICAL, flag 9) whose 'greatest extent practicable' qualifier appears dozens of times (flag 4 PRESENT) and which carries the same undefined presidential exemption-petition mechanism found in its companion fair-housing order, EO12892 (this chunk, 25.0%, Sec.6-605) -- issued four days apart, both establishing landmark equity-oriented federal frameworks with structurally identical discretionary-exemption escape valves.

title: Executive Order 13022—Administration of the Midway Islands

flags.justification: Transfers jurisdiction of the Midway Islands from the Navy to the Secretary of the Interior, who 'shall be responsible for the civil administration of the Midway Islands and all executive and legislative authority necessary for that administration, and all judicial authority respecting the Midway Islands' other than a narrow statutory carve-out (Sec.3(b)) - an unusually sweeping textual concentration of all three governance functions (executive, legislative, judicial) in a single executive-branch official over U.S. territory.
Sec.6 expressly preserves the Attorney General's authority to represent U.S. legal interests in civil/criminal cases arising under 48 U.S.C. 644a - a specific, named accountability preservation.
No existing oversight is eliminated; pending civil/criminal proceedings remain under Navy jurisdiction (Sec.4) while future matters transfer cleanly to Interior.

Establishes the Midway Atoll National Wildlife Refuge under the EO12996 National Wildlife Refuge System framework (this stream: 25.0%, Chunk 7) and the EO11593 historic-preservation policy (multi-coder corpus only, not stream-b-scored) - converting the historic Battle of Midway site into a permanent conservation and historic-preservation unit, a real and lasting change in the island group's legal and administrative status.

title: Executive Order 13026—Administration of Export Controls on Encryption Products

flags.justification: Licensing-review authority for transferred encryption items remains distributed across the interagency Operating Committee/ACEP structure established by EO12981 (this stream: 16.67%) and amended by EO13020 (this same batch), with the Department of Justice specifically elevated to full voting-member status (Sec.1(b)) rather than authority being concentrated in a single office.

Sec.1(a) disapplies the Export Administration Act's foreign-availability decontrol provisions (EAA Sec.4(c), 6(h)(2)-(4)) specifically for encryption products - removing the evidentiary check that would otherwise let exporters press for decontrol by demonstrating comparable products are available from foreign sources, a genuine accountability-mechanism removal layered on top of the judicial-review preclusion below.

The Secretary of Commerce 'may, in his discretion, consider the foreign availability of comparable encryption products... but is not required to issue licenses... based on such consideration' (Sec.1(a)) - an entirely unreviewable, standardless discretionary power - and Sec.1(e)'s list of 'appropriate controls' is expressly illustrative and open-ended ('may include, but are not limited to').

Sec.1(a) contains an explicit Presidential determination that facts concerning the foreign availability of encryption products 'cannot be made subject to public disclosure or judicial review without revealing or implicating classified information' - a direct, substantive foreclosure of judicial review and public disclosure going well beyond routine 'no right of judicial review' boilerplate found elsewhere in this corpus; this specific provision became the direct target of major First Amendment litigation (Bernstein v. DOJ, Junger v. Daley) challenging encryption export controls as unconstitutional prior restraint on speech.

This order is the central administrative instrument of the mid-1990s 'Crypto Wars,' triggering a decade of First Amendment litigation over encryption-as-speech and shaping U.S. cryptography export policy until the eventual 1999-2000 liberalization - one of the most legally consequential and extensively litigated national-security-versus-technology orders of the era.

Sec.1(c) declares that encryption software, unlike other export-controlled items, 'shall not be considered or treated as "technology"' under the EAA/EAR's own statutory definitions specifically to preserve export controls that would not otherwise apply under that classification - a definitional carve-out engineered around ordinary statutory categorization rather than a substantive rule change.

Not applicable - single coherent subject (administration of encryption-product export controls); the EO12981/EO13020 procedural amendments are a natural implementing sub-component of that single subject.
Not applicable - no perverse-incentive/zombie-emergency structure at issue distinct from the underlying EO12924 IEEPA emergency this order operates under.
Not applicable - a standing administrative framework tied to the ongoing EO12924 national emergency, not a separately time-limited grant.
Not applicable - governs federal export-licensing administration, not a third-party incentive-gap structure of the kind this flag targets.
Not applicable - State, Defense, Energy, Justice, and Commerce roles are cleanly coordinated through the existing OC/ACEP/Board appeal structure with no overlapping claims.

notes: Issued under the ongoing EO12924 IEEPA emergency (dual-use export control lapse), this order is the central instrument of the Clinton administration's mid-1990s encryption export-control regime - the flashpoint of the 'Crypto Wars.' Scores 58.33%, among the highest in this stream's corpus to date, driven by two CRITICAL findings: an explicit Presidential determination foreclosing public disclosure and judicial review of foreign-availability facts (Flag7, the direct target of Bernstein v. DOJ and Junger v. Daley), and the order's outsized, still-studied second-order legal and policy significance (Flag9) - reinforced by a genuine accountability-mechanism removal (Flag2, disapplying EAA foreign-availability decontrol checks), unreviewable Secretary-of-Commerce discretion (Flag4), and a definitional 'not technology' exemption carve-out (Flag11).

title: Executive Order 13031—Federal Alternative Fueled Vehicle Leadership

flags.justification: Implementation responsibility is distributed across every federal agency's own fleet managers, with DOE and GSA in a coordinating (not concentrating) role.

Agencies failing to meet statutory AFV percentage targets must submit 'an explanation for such failure and a plan... for achieving compliance' (Sec.2(b)), and DOE/GSA jointly report to OMB annually (Sec.2(c)) - a genuine, specific accountability mechanism for noncompliance.

Unlike many policy-guidance orders in this batch, the core acquisition requirement is a specific statutory percentage schedule (25% FY1996 rising to 75% FY1999+, Sec.1(a)) rather than an undefined discretionary standard.

Sec.5's vehicle-reporting-credit system - counting each medium-duty/zero-emissions vehicle as two light-duty AFVs and each heavy-duty AFV as three - creates a genuine gaming incentive: agencies can satisfy their percentage-based AFV acquisition targets by acquiring a small number of credit-multiplied vehicles rather than proportionally converting their broader fleets, without any anti-gaming safeguard specified in the order itself.
No existing oversight is eliminated; the order adds new annual OMB-directed compliance reporting (Sec.2).

Establishes a durable federal alternative-fuel-vehicle leadership framework that remains the direct institutional ancestor of the modern federal EV-fleet-conversion mandates still in effect decades later.

Notably, unlike its predecessor EO12844 (this stream: 37.5%, containing a flagged Sec.8 DOD/Treasury/DOJ self-determination carve-out), this order's only exemption category - law-enforcement, emergency, and Secretary-of-Defense-certified national-security vehicles (Sec.3) - derives directly from the underlying Energy Policy Act itself, and each agency must affirmatively 'include in its report an explanation of why an exemption is claimed' rather than self-determine unreviewably.
Not applicable - single coherent subject (federal alternative-fueled-vehicle fleet acquisition).
Not applicable - a standing statutory-implementation program, not a time-limited grant.
Not applicable - governs internal federal fleet acquisition, not a third-party incentive-gap structure of the kind this flag targets.
Not applicable - DOE and GSA roles are cleanly divided (technical assistance vs. procurement/leasing coordination, Sec.4(c)).

notes: Supersedes EO12844 (Federal Use of Alternative Fueled Vehicles, this stream: 37.5%, Batch 9 Chunk 1) with a more detailed, statutorily-grounded implementation framework for the Energy Policy Act of 1992's AFV acquisition mandates. Scores 14.29% - notably lower than its predecessor - via a genuine diff-aware structural improvement: the predecessor's flagged open-ended self-exemption clause (Sec.8, allowing DOD/Treasury/DOJ to self-determine applicability) is absent here, replaced by a statutorily-grounded exemption category requiring affirmative justification (Flag11 ABSENT vs. predecessor's PRESENT finding). The one genuine new finding is Flag5: Sec.5's 2x/3x vehicle-reporting-credit multiplier system creates a real, unaddressed gaming incentive for agencies to satisfy AFV percentage targets without proportional broader-fleet conversion.

title: Executive Order 13045—Protection of Children From Environmental Health Risks and Safety Risks

flags.justification: Establishes a broad 17-member Task Force with HHS/EPA co-chairs and extensive cross-agency membership - a distributed coordinating body, not a concentration point.

title: Executive Order 13067—Blocking Sudanese Government Property and Prohibiting Transactions With Sudan

flags.justification: Vests broad blocking authority over 'all property and interests in property of the Government of Sudan that are in the United States, that hereafter come within the United States, or that hereafter come within the possession or control of United States persons' (Sec.1) - open-ended in both temporal and definitional scope, structurally similar to EO12170's (Carter-era Iran order) blocking-authority pattern.

A newly-declared national emergency ('constitute an unusual and extraordinary threat... and hereby declare a national emergency') with no textual sunset or built-in review trigger - a genuine zombie-emergency-trap risk instance at the moment of declaration, structurally identical in form to the pattern first documented at EO12170 and subsequently at EO12957/EO12959 (this stream) - this Sudan sanctions program in fact remained active for nearly two decades before its eventual partial easing.
Contains no expiration date, review requirement, or automatic termination trigger - textually open-ended by design, relying entirely on the National Emergencies Act's annual-renewal mechanism.

Congress's statutory NEA oversight tools are not displaced by the order's text, even though - consistent with the broader pattern this stream has documented - they were not exercised to terminate this emergency for many years.

The founding order of the U.S. Sudan sanctions regime, directly tied to Sudan's support for international terrorism, destabilization of neighboring governments, and human-rights violations including slavery and religious-freedom denial - this program remained the operative Sudan sanctions framework for nearly two decades, closely tied to the subsequent Darfur genocide response and one of the longest-running IEEPA sanctions programs in the corpus.

Sec.3 provides two narrowly-defined exceptions - official U.S./UN government business and journalistic activity - a defined, specifically-bounded exemption architecture rather than an open-ended discretionary regime.

title: Executive Order 13083—Federalism

flags.justification: Directs agencies to adhere to federalism principles limiting federal preemption of state policymaking discretion; if anything constrains rather than concentrates federal agency authority.

Sec.4(b)(2) requires agencies to publicly disclose, in the Federal Register preamble, the extent of their prior consultation with state/local governments and provide written communications to OMB - a genuine, specific transparency mechanism.

Bundles four substantively distinct components into a single order: a fundamental federalism-principles statement (Sec.2), detailed policymaking criteria for when federal action is justified (Sec.3), a formal state/local consultation process (Sec.4), and a separate state/local regulatory-waiver-streamlining process (Sec.5).

The consultation and waiver-flexibility obligations are pervasively hedged with 'to the extent practicable and permitted by law' and 'as appropriate,' with no defined consequence specified if an agency simply fails to consult meaningfully.
No existing oversight is eliminated; the order supplements rather than supersedes EO12866 and EO12988 (Sec.7(b)).

One of the most instructive procedural-legitimacy case studies in this entire corpus: issued May 14, 1998, this order was suspended by EO13095 (this same chunk) less than three months later, on August 5, 1998, after state and local government organizations protested that the order itself had been drafted without the 'full and adequate consultation' it was meant to require of federal agencies going forward - a rare, documented, real-time instance of an order being pulled for failing to meet its own stated standard.
Federal agencies retain their existing statutory authorities; no competing claims are created.
Not applicable - no perverse-incentive structure at issue.
Not applicable - no built-in sunset; the order's termination came via a separate suspension order (EO13095, this same chunk) rather than a textual sunset.
Not applicable - governs federal-state regulatory relations, not third-party incentive structures.
Not applicable - no discretionary exemptions regime; the waiver-streamlining process (Sec.5) is captured under Flag4 as a vague-standard issue.

notes: Revokes EO12612 (this stream: 50.0%) and EO12875 (this stream: 33.33%), replacing them with a new federalism-principles and consultation framework. Scores 28.57% - driven by genuine bundling of four distinct components (Flag3), pervasive undefined consultation/waiver standards (Flag4), and a CRITICAL Flag9 finding reflecting the order's extraordinary meta-irony: it was suspended by its own author (EO13095, this same chunk) within three months for failing to meet the very state/local consultation standard it purported to establish - one of this stream's clearest documented examples of a structural-legitimacy failure manifesting in real time.

title: Executive Order 13085—Establishment of the Enrichment Oversight Committee

flags.justification: A 10-agency Enrichment Oversight Committee (EOC), NSC-chaired, with a further HEU Agreement Oversight Subcommittee - broadly distributed rather than concentrated.

The EOC must report semiannually to the Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs and prepare the annual report for statutory transmittal to Congress under the USEC Privatization Act (Sec.3(a)) - a genuine, specific, and notably NOT carved-out Congressional reporting requirement.

The EOC's information-collection mandate over the privatized USEC includes proprietary financial and strategic-plan data collected 'as necessary to fulfill the EOC's oversight functions' (Sec.6(d)) - an inherently open-ended necessity standard governing collection of a private corporation's competitively-sensitive information.

No existing oversight is eliminated; the EOC's role is explicitly coordinative, converying other agencies' views to the NRC on regulatory decisions (Sec.7) rather than displacing NRC's own licensing authority.

Oversees implementation of the 1993 U.S.-Russia HEU Agreement (the 'Megatons to Megawatts' program), one of the most significant nuclear nonproliferation and disarmament programs in modern history, ultimately converting the equivalent of approximately 20,000 dismantled Soviet/Russian nuclear warheads' worth of highly enriched uranium into commercial reactor fuel over roughly two decades.

Not applicable - single coherent subject (post-privatization enrichment-industry national-security oversight), with the HEU Subcommittee and FOCI-monitoring functions being natural implementing sub-components.

title: Executive Order 13088—Blocking Property of the Governments of the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia (Serbia and Montenegro), the Republic of Serbia, and the Republic of Montenegro, and Prohibiting New Investment in the Republic of Serbia in Response to the Situation in Kosovo

flags.justification: Vests broad blocking authority over 'all property and interests in property' of the Yugoslav federal, Serbian, and Montenegrin governments 'that are in the United States, that hereafter come within the United States, or that are or hereinafter come within the possession or control of United States persons' (Sec.1) - open-ended in temporal and definitional scope.

title: Executive Order 13101—Greening the Government Through Waste Prevention, Recycling, and Federal Acquisition

flags.justification: Creates a multi-tier bureaucratic hierarchy - a Steering Committee (CEQ/FEE/OFPP), a Federal Environmental Executive with government-wide compliance-monitoring authority, a Task Force, and Agency Environmental Executives at every major procuring agency (Sec.103, Part 3) - with the FEE given fairly centralized coordination and compliance-monitoring authority across the entire executive branch's environmental-procurement apparatus.

The core 100-percent-compliance mandate for EPA-designated recycled-content items is subject to an agency-self-determined 'written justification' escape valve - a product 'not available competitively... within a reasonable time frame,' 'does not meet appropriate performance standards,' or 'only available at an unreasonable price' (Sec.402(c)) - with no external review of that self-determination specified in the order's text.

title: Executive Order 13116—Identification of Trade Expansion Priorities and Discriminatory Procurement Practices

flags.justification: Delegates statutory USTR authority already vested by the Trade Act of 1974 and Trade Agreements Act of 1979; not a new concentration of authority.

Both Part I and Part II require the Trade Representative to submit reports directly to named Congressional committees (Finance/Ways and Means; Governmental Affairs/Government Reform and Oversight) and publish findings in the Federal Register - genuine, specific, non-carved-out Congressional accountability.

Bundles two textually parallel but substantively distinct statutory investigation regimes into a single order: Part I's Trade Expansion Priorities identification/negotiation/investigation process (Sec.1-3) and Part II's Discriminatory Government Procurement Practices identification/investigation process (Sec.1-5).

Sec.2(c) of Part II permits the Trade Representative to consider 'any other additional criteria deemed appropriate' beyond the itemized statutory factors - an open-ended residual discretion clause layered atop an otherwise statutorily-anchored framework.

Part I Sec.1(c)'s discretion to omit from the report 'foreign country practices that were not identified because they are already being addressed by provisions of United States trade law, existing bilateral trade agreements, or in trade negotiations with other countries and progress is being made toward their elimination' creates a perverse incentive for foreign governments to engage in cosmetic, ongoing 'progress' rather than genuine resolution, since USTR's soft, subjective 'progress is being made' standard lets a country avoid formal priority identification and the harder Sec.3 investigation track without actually eliminating the practice.

Both Part I Sec.1(a) and Part II Sec.1(a) bound the identification/reporting mandate to the National Trade Estimate Reports 'for 1999, 2000, and 2001' -- a genuine, dated three-year window after which the order's own operative directive lapses without further executive action, rather than establishing an indefinite or permanent USTR reporting obligation.
No existing oversight is eliminated; the order adds, rather than removes, structured Congressional reporting obligations.

Part III Sec.2 requires the Trade Representative to 'seek information and advice from U.S. businesses in the United States and in the countries involved in the practices under consideration' -- a genuine, built-in third-party input channel: U.S. businesses harmed by foreign trade barriers have both standing and direct incentive to surface those barriers to USTR.
Implements the recurring Special 301/Title III trade-enforcement mechanisms that remain part of USTR's ongoing annual trade-priorities practice.
Sole USTR administration with no competing agency claims.

Part I Sec.1(c) gives the Trade Representative unbounded discretion to decline to identify a foreign country practice as a 'priority foreign country practice' -- and thereby exempt it from the Sec.3 mandatory-investigation track -- whenever USTR subjectively determines the practice is 'already being addressed' by existing law, agreements, or negotiations and 'progress is being made toward their elimination'; a broad, single-actor-controlled carve-out with no defined objective criteria or external check on what counts as sufficient 'progress.'

notes: Directs the U.S. Trade Representative to annually identify priority foreign trade barriers and discriminatory government-procurement practices for 1999-2001, implementing recurring Trade Act Section 301/Title III mechanisms. Scores 21.43% driven by genuine bundling of two parallel statutory investigation regimes (Flag3), an open-ended residual-discretion clause (Flag4), and ongoing significance to USTR trade-enforcement practice (Flag9) - offset by genuine, specific, non-carved-out Congressional reporting requirements built into both regimes (Flag2 ABSENT).

Unusually well-specified for an order of this length: delegations are itemized by exact statutory citation throughout rather than governed by open-ended discretionary standards.

title: Executive Order 13123—Greening the Government Through Efficient Energy Management

flags.justification: Creates an elaborate multi-tier hierarchy - OMB's Deputy Director for Management holding centralized agency-scorecard evaluation and scoring authority (Sec.306(a)), DOE's Federal Energy Management Program, the President's Management Council, the Interagency Energy Policy Committee, the Interagency Energy Management Task Force, and agency-level Senior Officials plus dedicated Energy Teams (Sec.304-305) - a genuinely centralized, multi-layered energy-governance structure spanning the entire executive branch.

Sec.303 requires detailed annual agency reports to the President beginning in 2000, feeding OMB's public agency energy scorecards (Sec.306(a)(1)) - a robust, genuinely transparent accountability mechanism, among the more accountability-conscious orders in this batch.

Bundles an extraordinarily broad set of components across seven parts: numeric greenhouse-gas/energy/water reduction goals (Part 2), organizational accountability structures (Part 3), fourteen distinct technical strategies spanning life-cycle costing, ENERGY STAR procurement, sustainable building design, off-grid generation, and electricity purchasing (Part 4), agency-specific technical-assistance directives to OMB/DOE/GSA/DOD (Part 5), and general provisions including a waiver process (Part 6).

The 'life-cycle cost-effective' qualifier - undefined beyond a cross-reference to DOE cost-effectiveness regulations - and 'as appropriate'/'to the maximum extent practicable' hedging condition virtually every numeric target and operative duty in the order, including the headline 30-percent greenhouse-gas-reduction goal itself (Sec.201).
No existing oversight is eliminated; the order adds a new, more rigorous OMB scorecard-evaluation layer atop pre-existing energy-management statutes.

A foundational, landmark federal sustainability order establishing the OMB agency-energy-scorecard evaluation system that persisted for years and directly informed subsequent 'greening government' orders (EO13423 of 2007, EO13514 of 2009) - among the most enduringly influential administrative-reform orders in the entire Clinton-era corpus, structurally paired with EO13101 (Greening the Government Through Waste Prevention, this stream, Chunk 10, 37.5%).
OMB, DOE/FEMP, GSA, and DOD roles are each clearly, non-competingly assigned specific coordination functions (Sec.306, 308).

Sec.602's agency waiver provision - 'the agency may ask DOE for a waiver of the provision' - is a genuine but externally-reviewed mechanism (DOE, a third party, must grant the waiver and report all granted waivers in its annual Congressional FEMP report), notably more accountability-conscious than this stream's previously-tracked self-determined exemption patterns.

title: Executive Order 13129—Blocking Property and Prohibiting Transactions With the Taliban

flags.justification: Vests open-ended blocking authority over 'all property and interests in property' of the Taliban and any Treasury-designated persons found to be 'owned or controlled by, or to act for or on behalf of, the Taliban' or to provide support to it (Sec.1) - broad, temporally open-ended scope.

A newly-declared national emergency ('constitute an unusual and extraordinary threat... and hereby declare a national emergency') with no textual sunset - a genuine zombie-emergency-trap risk instance, the fourth confirmed instance of a new IEEPA blocking-order declaration in this stream, after EO12957 (Iran: 50.0%), EO13067 (Sudan: 50.0%, Chunk 9), and EO13088 (Kosovo: 50.0%, Chunk 10).
Contains no expiration date, review requirement, or automatic termination trigger.

One of the most historically consequential orders in this entire stream: declares the founding U.S. national emergency and sanctions regime against the Taliban specifically for 'allowing territory under its control in Afghanistan to be used as a safe haven and base of operations for Usama bin Ladin and the Al-Qaida organization' - issued July 4, 1999, more than two years before the September 11, 2001 attacks, directly foreshadowing the October 2001 U.S. invasion of Afghanistan and the subsequent two-decade War on Terror. This is the first order in this stream's corpus to name the Taliban as the primary sanctioned party specifically for harboring bin Ladin and Al-Qaida.

Given the subsequent history of September 11, 2001 and the War on Terror, this may be the single most consequential order encountered anywhere in this stream's corpus to date, scored here purely on its own textual structure two years before that history unfolded.

title: Executive Order 13132—Federalism

flags.justification: Directs agencies to adhere to federalism principles limiting federal preemption of state policymaking discretion; if anything constrains rather than concentrates federal agency authority.

Sec.8 ('Accountability') requires each agency's designated federalism official to certify to OMB, on every draft regulation or legislative proposal with federalism implications, that the order's consultation requirements have been meaningfully met - a more explicit, formalized certification mechanism than EO13083's predecessor disclosure requirement.

Bundles seven substantively distinct components into a single order: fundamental federalism principles (Sec.2), detailed policymaking criteria (Sec.3), special preemption requirements (Sec.4), legislative-proposal restrictions (Sec.5), a formal consultation process (Sec.6), a waiver-streamlining process with a defined 120-day decision deadline (Sec.7), and a certification-based accountability structure (Sec.8).

The consultation and waiver-flexibility obligations remain pervasively hedged with 'to the extent practicable and permitted by law,' with no defined consequence specified if an agency's certification of adequate consultation proves inaccurate.
Sec.10(a) explicitly supplements rather than supersedes EO12372, EO12866, and EO12988.

The historically significant successor and consolidator that closes the loop first opened by EO13083 (this stream: 28.57% CRITICAL, Chunk 10, suspended by EO13095 this stream: 16.67%, Chunk 10, within three months for inadequate consultation): Sec.10(b) simultaneously revokes EO12612 (this stream: 50.0%), EO12875 (this stream: 33.33%), EO13083, AND EO13095 - consolidating three generations of federalism policy into a single framework that, after more deliberate consultation than its predecessor, remained the operative federal federalism-executive-order framework for roughly the next decade and a half and remains one of the most frequently cited executive orders in federal preemption litigation.

Sec.8(c) requires OMB and the Assistant to the President for Intergovernmental Affairs to jointly confer with state/local officials to verify proper implementation, avoiding the ambiguity of overlapping unassigned responsibility.

Sec.7's waiver-streamlining process, while genuinely more accountability-conscious than EO13083's version (adding a defined 120-day decision deadline and a written-notice-of-reasons requirement for denials, Sec.7(c)), remains agency-self-administered discretionary relief from otherwise-applicable statutory or regulatory requirements.
Not applicable - no perverse-incentive structure at issue.
Not applicable - no built-in sunset; a standing federalism-policy framework.
Not applicable - governs federal-state regulatory relations, not third-party incentive structures.

notes: IMPORTANT CROSS-REFERENCE: the long-anticipated final resolution of the federalism-consultation saga tracked across this stream since Chunk 10 - simultaneously revokes EO12612 (50.0%), EO12875 (33.33%), EO13083 (28.57% CRITICAL), and EO13095 (16.67%), replacing all four with a single, more elaborately consulted and more formally accountable framework. What changes in kind rather than in raw percentage is Flag9's significance driver: from a CRITICAL finding rooted in documented process failure at EO13083, to a CRITICAL finding rooted in this order's status as a durable, carefully-consulted, still-influential foundational framework that governed federal-state relations for roughly the next decade and a half.

title: Executive Order 13139—Improving Health Protection of Military Personnel Participating in Particular Military Operations

flags.justification: Centralizes ultimate informed-consent-waiver decision authority in the President personally, with the Secretary of Defense's waiver-request authority explicitly made non-delegable (Sec.3(d): 'The Secretary may not delegate the authority to make this waiver request') - an unusually concentrated single-point decision structure for a matter directly affecting servicemembers' medical rights.
Sec.4(b)-(c) require prompt Congressional notification per statute and public Federal Register notice of each individual waiver determination - genuine, concrete transparency obligations.

Bundles four substantively distinct components: the investigational-drug countermeasure development process (Sec.2), the multi-step informed-consent-waiver request/certification/decision process (Sec.3), post-waiver ongoing monitoring and reporting requirements (Sec.4), and mandatory training/health-risk-communication requirements (Sec.5).

The Presidential waiver criteria themselves ('not feasible,' 'contrary to the best interests of the member,' 'not in the interests of national security,' Sec.3(b)) are broad discretionary standards, compounded by an escape valve permitting the Secretary to bypass full FDA-standards certification where 'highly impracticable' (Sec.3(d)(2)(B)).

Each waiver 'will expire at the end of 1 year (or an alternative time period... not to exceed 1 year)... or when the Secretary informs the President that the particular military operation... has ended, whichever is earlier' (Sec.4(d)) - a genuine, dated, renewable sunset per waiver instance.
No existing oversight is eliminated; the order affirmatively adds ongoing DoD Inspector General and FDA monitoring requirements (Sec.4(a)) atop the existing 10 U.S.C. 1107 framework.

Directly responds to the intense controversy surrounding the Anthrax Vaccine Immunization Program and the broader history of investigational drugs administered to Gulf War-era troops without full informed consent - establishes the formal, multi-layered Presidential-waiver governance process for future use, an extraordinarily significant military-medical-ethics framework directly connected to this stream's previously-tracked Gulf War Illness accountability chain (EO13034: 12.5%, Chunk 9; EO13075: 18.75%, Chunk 10).
DoD, FDA, NSC (APNSA), and OSTP (APST) roles in the multi-step review-and-certification process are clearly, non-competingly delineated (Sec.3(e)-(i)).

notes: Establishes the formal Presidential process for waiving informed-consent requirements before administering investigational drugs to deployed military personnel, directly responding to the Anthrax Vaccine Immunization Program controversy. title: Executive Order 13141—Environmental Review of Trade Agreements

title: Executive Order 13148—Greening the Government Through Leadership in Environmental Management

flags.justification: Vests EPA's Administrator with centralized compliance-monitoring authority across the entire executive branch, including authority to conduct reviews/inspections, demand corrective compliance plans from noncompliant agencies (Sec.406(b)), and report annually to the President and the public on agency compliance (Sec.406(c)) - the most centralized enforcement-style hierarchy among this stream's three 'Greening the Government' orders.

Establishes a 14-member Commission (state/local workforce officials, economists, labor organizations, business leaders, Members of Congress) to study economic dislocation from trade/technology/globalization and worker/community adjustment strategies.

Not applicable - advisory commission, no operational/incentive authority.
Explicit termination 30 days after submitting its report (Sec.5) - a clean, dated sunset.

Sec.4(b) directs the Secretary of Labor to perform the President's FACA administrative functions 'except that of reporting to the Congress' - the eighteenth confirmed instance in this stream's corpus of the recurring FACA carve-out template. NOTABLE NUANCE: unlike most such commissions, Sec.3 explicitly directs the Commission's actual substantive final report to be submitted 'to the President and the Congress' directly - meaning the carve-out here applies only to routine FACA administrative paperwork, while the Commission's core deliverable does reach Congress.

title: Executive Order 13175—Consultation and Coordination With Indian Tribal Governments

flags.justification: Directs agencies to respect tribal self-government and sovereignty; constrains rather than concentrates federal agency authority.

Sec.7 ('Accountability') requires each agency's designated tribal-consultation official to certify to OMB, on every draft regulation or legislative proposal with tribal implications, that the order's consultation requirements have been meaningfully met - directly mirroring EO13132's Sec.8 certification structure and notably more formalized than EO13084's predecessor disclosure requirement.

Bundles six substantively distinct components: fundamental tribal-sovereignty principles (Sec.2), detailed policymaking criteria (Sec.3), legislative-proposal restrictions (Sec.4), a formal consultation process (Sec.5), a waiver-streamlining process with a defined 120-day decision deadline (Sec.6), and a certification-based accountability structure (Sec.7).

The consultation and waiver-flexibility obligations remain pervasively hedged with 'to the extent practicable and permitted by law,' with no defined consequence specified if an agency's certification of adequate consultation proves inaccurate.

Sec.9(a) explicitly supplements rather than supersedes EO12866 and EO12988; Sec.9(b) explicitly states this order 'complement[s]' the consultation and waiver provisions of EO13132 (Federalism, this stream: 31.25% CRITICAL, this same batch) - a deliberate, cross-referential integration between the two companion consultation frameworks.

The direct companion and successor to EO13084 (this stream: 21.43%, Chunk 10), revoking it (Sec.9(c)) and replacing it with a more elaborate, more formally accountable government-to-government tribal-consultation framework explicitly coordinated with EO13132 (Federalism, this stream, this same batch) - together these two orders form a matched pair of foundational consultation frameworks (for states and for tribes, respectively) that would remain the operative federal executive orders on their respective subjects for roughly the next two decades.

Sec.7(c) requires OMB and the Assistant to the President for Intergovernmental Affairs to jointly confer with tribal officials to verify implementation, directly mirroring EO13132's implementation-verification structure.

Sec.6's waiver-streamlining process, while genuinely more accountability-conscious than EO13084's version (adding a defined 120-day decision deadline and a written-notice-of-reasons requirement for denials), remains agency-self-administered discretionary relief from otherwise-applicable statutory or regulatory requirements.
Not applicable - no perverse-incentive structure at issue.
Not applicable - no built-in sunset; a standing tribal-consultation-policy framework.
Not applicable - governs federal-tribal regulatory relations, not third-party incentive structures.

notes: IMPORTANT CROSS-REFERENCE: revokes and replaces EO13084 (this stream: 21.43%, Chunk 10), the earlier tribal-consultation companion order to EO13083 (Federalism)/EO13095 (this stream, Chunk 10). Scores 31.25% - matching EO13132 (Federalism, this stream: 31.25% CRITICAL, this same batch) EXACTLY, a striking independent structural convergence between two companion consultation-framework orders addressing different constituencies (states versus tribes) via parallel mechanisms (fundamental principles, policymaking criteria, formal consultation process, time-bound waiver-streamlining, and OMB-certification accountability). Both orders explicitly cross-reference and 'complement' each other (Sec.9(b)), forming a matched pair that governed federal-state and federal-tribal consultation policy for roughly the following two decades.

title: Executive Order 13176—Facilitation of a Presidential Transition

flags.justification: A 12-member Presidential Transition Coordinating Council, Chief-of-Staff-chaired, drawing on Cabinet Secretary/Counsel/OMB/FBI/OPM/GSA/Archivist/IRS/OGE representation - broadly representative, not a concentration point.
No accountability gap identified; a purely facilitative/informational coordination order.

Bundles five distinct informational/coordination deliverables into a single order: Council establishment and coordination (Sec.1), orientation-activity coordination for prospective appointees (Sec.2(a)), a transition directory covering all department/agency authorities (Sec.2(b)), a comprehensive catalogue of all Senate-confirmed positions (Sec.2(c)), and agency-level orientation-materials preparation (Sec.2(d)).
No existing oversight is eliminated.

A genuine, historically significant institutionalization of presidential-transition support machinery, issued amid the unusually contentious and prolonged 2000 Bush v. Gore transition period, with lasting influence on subsequent transition-support practice that was eventually further codified by later Pre-Election Presidential Transition Act amendments.
GSA, OPM, OGE, and Archivist roles in Sec.2's coordination activities are clearly, non-competingly delineated.

Not applicable - the order's informational deliverables (transition directory contents, PAS-position-catalogue contents) are each specifically itemized rather than governed by an open-ended discretionary standard.
Not applicable - no perverse-incentive structure at issue.
Not applicable - a one-time facilitative order tied to the 2000-2001 transition, no separate sunset provision specified.
Not applicable - governs internal federal transition-support coordination, not third-party incentive structures.
Not applicable - no discretionary exemptions regime created.

notes: Establishes the Presidential Transition Coordinating Council and directs preparation of transition-support materials, issued amid the historically contentious 2000 Bush v. Gore transition period. Scores 16.67% (6-flag applicable denominator, since Flag4 is NOT_APPLICABLE here) - one of the lowest-scoring orders in this chunk - driven solely by genuine bundling of five itemized informational deliverables (Flag3) and real institutional significance to presidential-transition practice (Flag9), with an unusually low structural-risk profile given the order's concretely-specified, non-discretionary deliverables (Flag4 NOT_APPLICABLE).

title: Executive Order 13177—National Commission on the Use of Offsets in Defense Trade and President's Council on the Use of Offsets in Commercial Trade

flags.justification: The statutory Commission requires Congressional-leadership concurrence for Presidential appointments (Sec.1) - notably reducing rather than concentrating appointment authority - while the parallel Council shares the Commission's membership.

NOTABLE POSITIVE CONTRAST: the statutory Commission 'will report to the appropriate congressional committees' DIRECTLY (Sec.3) - a genuine, non-carved-out statutory Congress-reporting requirement, unlike the great majority of this batch's FACA advisory bodies.
Both the Commission's and Council's duties are concretely itemized (Sec.2(a)-(c), Sec.6) rather than governed by open-ended discretionary standards.

The Commission terminates 30 days after transmitting its statutory report (Sec.4(d)); the Council terminates on the date of that same report's transmission (Sec.8(b)) - clean, dated sunsets for both bodies.

NOTABLE SAME-ORDER CONTRAST: while the statutory Commission reports directly to Congress with no carve-out (Flag2 ABSENT above), the parallel non-statutory Council - created by this order alone (Sec.5-8) - DOES carry the standard FACA carve-out language ('except that of reporting to the Congress,' Sec.8(a)) - the nineteenth confirmed instance of this recurring template, notably appearing in the same order as a genuine non-carved-out statutory body, an instructive direct contrast within a single order.

Addresses a genuinely significant, if narrower, defense-trade-policy issue (offset requirements in international arms sales) with real implications for U.S. defense-industrial-base competitiveness, nonproliferation policy, and dependency on foreign nations for critical components.

The statutory Commission and the executive-created Council have clearly delineated, non-competing roles, with the Council explicitly directed to 'consult with and... provide information to the Commission' (Sec.6).
Not applicable - single coherent subject (defense and commercial trade offsets policy), notwithstanding the dual Commission/Council structure.
Not applicable - no perverse-incentive structure at issue.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure of the kind this flag targets.
Not applicable - no discretionary exemptions regime created.

notes: Implements a statutory National Commission on the Use of Offsets in Defense Trade (Public Law 106-113) and creates a parallel, non-statutory President's Council on the Use of Offsets in Commercial Trade. Scores 21.43% via dated sunsets for both bodies (Flag6) and the nineteenth confirmed FACA carve-out instance for the Council (Flag7) - notably contrasted, within this same order, against the statutory Commission's genuine, non-carved-out direct reporting obligation to Congress (Flag2 ABSENT), a clean illustration of the structural difference between Congressionally-created and Presidentially-created advisory bodies.

title: Executive Order 13178—Northwestern Hawaiian Islands Coral Reef Ecosystem Reserve

flags.justification: The Secretary of Commerce is granted broad, largely unilateral management authority over a 1,200x100nm reserve, including power to issue 'any regulations... the Secretary determines are necessary' (Sec.5(b)(11)) and to make 'technical modifications' to boundaries (Sec.6(c)); the Reserve Council (established under NMSA Sec.315, a FACA body) is purely advisory.

The order's only mandated report (a 1-year implementation progress report, Sec.5(g)) goes solely to the Chair of the Council on Environmental Quality - an internal executive-branch recipient, not Congress or the public.
Not applicable - single coherent subject (coral reef ecosystem conservation).

Despite broad discretionary management authority, the operative restrictions are concretely itemized: specific fishing-permit caps by type, precise mean-depth boundaries (10/20/50fm) for named Reserve Preservation Areas, and an enumerated list of prohibited activities (Sec.7(b)).
Not applicable - no emergency declaration or exploitable incentive structure; permit caps are fixed against historical baselines, not an ongoing gaming dynamic.

No sunset or automatic-termination provision for the Reserve or the Secretary's management authority; several Preservation Areas are explicitly temporary 'until made permanent after adequate public review and comment' (Sec.8(a)), but the order creates no expiration on its own authority - notable given the order later required a dedicated follow-up (EO13196) simply to complete that public-comment process.

No independent-oversight-blocking language; the boilerplate judicial-review disclaimer (Sec.11(g)) is standard across virtually all orders in this corpus and does not itself curtail external review of implementation.
Not applicable - a regulatory/land-use regime, not a third-party grant or contract program.

Established what was, at the time, one of the largest marine protected areas in the world (3.5 million acres, ~1,200nm), capping the entire Northwestern Hawaiian Islands commercial and recreational fishing industry and setting in motion the process that became the Papahanaumokuakea Marine National Monument - a precedent-setting, historically consequential action with sweeping economic and ecological second-order effects.

Extensive, carefully negotiated coordination with the Department of the Interior (Fish and Wildlife Service, Hawaiian Islands/Midway Atoll refuges) and the State of Hawaii, with explicit non-diminishment-of-jurisdiction language (Sec.10(b)) - a model of inter-agency coordination rather than cannibalization.

Multiple carved-out exemption categories: Native Hawaiian noncommercial subsistence/cultural/religious uses (Sec.9), a broad national-security and military-exercise carve-out determined unilaterally by the Secretary of Defense (Sec.11(c)), and an open-ended emergency-response exemption for threats to human health/safety or the marine environment.

notes: Establishes a 1,200x100nm coral reef reserve under NMSA Sec.315 with a FACA Reserve Council, detailed management/conservation measures, and fishing-effort caps. Scores 31.25% (5/16), driven by concentrated Secretarial management authority (Flag1), a CEQ-only (non-public/non-Congressional) progress report (Flag2), a CRITICAL second-order-effects finding (Flag9) given the reserve's unprecedented scale and its role as the direct precursor to the Papahanaumokuakea Marine National Monument, and a broad SecDef-determined national-security exemption (Flag11) - offset by concretely itemized (not vague) restrictions (Flag4 ABSENT) and strong inter-agency coordination (Flag10 ABSENT). Cross-references EO13089 (this stream: 25.0%) and EO13158 (this stream: 21.43%) for agency-responsibility/national-security-exemption consistency (Sec.11(b)-(c)).

title: Executive Order 13179—Providing Compensation to America's Nuclear Weapons Workers

flags.justification: The Secretary of Labor is granted 'primary responsibility for administering the Program' with broad authority to 'administer and decide all questions arising under the Act not assigned to other agencies' (Sec.2(a)(i)).

Genuinely robust, public-facing accountability: DOE must make claims-related data 'publicly available on at least an annual basis' (Sec.2(c)(vi)) including claim counts, illness findings, and successful-claim data, plus mandatory OMB budget-submission reporting (Sec.5) - a notably transparent design relative to most orders in this corpus.
Not applicable - single coherent subject (nuclear weapons worker compensation implementation).

Implementation is highly itemized with concrete statutory deadlines throughout (May 31 2001 x2, July 31 2001, Jan 15 2001, Jan 1 2001, March 1 2001) and specific delegated statutory sections, not open-ended discretion.
Not applicable - no emergency declaration; the order's incentive-design choices (see Flag8) are corrective, not exploitable.
Not applicable - an ongoing entitlement/compensation program, not a time-limited grant of authority.

No oversight-preemption language; an Interagency Working Group (Sec.3) is explicitly tasked with resolving implementation impediments, and a January 1, 2001 legislative-proposal deadline invites further Congressional involvement.

The order directly and explicitly corrects a documented third-party incentive problem: it recites that DOE and its predecessors previously had 'a past policy of encouraging and assisting DOE contractors in opposing the claims of workers' and now affirmatively directs DOE to instruct contractors 'not to contest' claims found valid by the physician panel (Sec.2(c)(v)(2)) - closing rather than creating a third-party incentive gap.

A historically significant compensation program for 'hundreds of thousands' of nuclear-weapons-complex workers exposed to beryllium and ionizing radiation since World War II, implementing the Energy Employees Occupational Illness Compensation Program Act of 2000 (P.L. 106-398) and reversing decades of DOE policy - broad, lasting effects across the federal workers'-compensation and nuclear-industrial landscape.

Clear, non-overlapping division of responsibilities among Labor (primary administration), HHS (dose reconstruction/Special Exposure Cohort), DOE (worker identification/State-claim assistance), and DOJ (RECA coordination), reinforced by a dedicated Interagency Working Group.
Not applicable - a benefits-expansion program, not a regulatory-prohibition order with exemption carve-outs.

notes: Implements the Energy Employees Occupational Illness Compensation Program Act of 2000, distributing responsibilities across Labor/HHS/DOE/DOJ and establishing an Interagency Working Group and a FACA-based Advisory Board on Radiation and Worker Health. Scores 21.43% (3/14), driven almost entirely by concentrated primary administrative authority in Labor (Flag1) and a CRITICAL historic-scale second-order-effects finding (Flag9), substantially offset by genuinely public claims-data reporting (Flag2 ABSENT), heavily itemized statutory deadlines (Flag4 ABSENT), and an explicit correction of the pre-existing contractor-incentive problem (Flag8 ABSENT). NOTABLE: the Advisory Board's FACA-administration language (Sec.2(b)(v)) conspicuously lacks the standard 'except that of reporting to the Congress' carve-out seen in ~20 other FACA committees this batch - a THIRD negative-control instance (after EO13017 and EO13134) where ordinary FACA Congressional-reporting obligations are not excused.

title: Executive Order 13180—Air Traffic Performance-Based Organization

flags.justification: Creates a new Chief Operating Officer position (per the Air-21 statute) heading the new Air Traffic Organization, reporting to but exercising substantial delegated operational authority over the entire air traffic control system.

The mandated evaluation report on the ATO's operation and effectiveness is due only after 5 years, and goes solely to 'the Secretary and the Administrator' (Sec.4) - no interim reporting and no Congressional/public transmittal requirement.
Not applicable - single coherent subject (FAA air traffic services reorganization).

The COO's 'measurable organization and individual goals' and performance targets (Sec.1(c)) are left entirely to negotiation between the COO and the Administrator, with only 'consultation' (not approval) by the Air Traffic Control Subcommittee - no criteria for adequate performance are specified in the order itself.

The COO 'may receive an annual bonus not to exceed 30 percent of the annual rate of basic pay, based upon the Administrator's evaluation of the COO's performance' (Sec.1(c)) - a self-referential, non-independently-verified pay-for-performance structure in which the same Administrator who created and oversees the ATO also grades and financially rewards its head, with no public disclosure of the underlying metrics.
Not applicable - a permanent organizational restructuring, no sunset expected.
The Aviation Management Advisory Committee retains a 'general oversight' role over the ATO (Sec.3); no oversight function is explicitly eliminated.
Not applicable - an internal government reorganization, not a third-party grant/contract program.

Significant restructuring of a safety-critical national function, though effects are meaningfully bounded by the order's express ring-fencing of the Administrator's 'general safety, security, and policymaking functions' as 'unaffected by this order' (Sec.1(b)).
A purely internal FAA reorganization; no competing inter-agency claims are implicated.
Not applicable - no exemption categories at issue.

notes: Establishes the FAA's performance-based Air Traffic Organization under a new Chief Operating Officer created by the Air-21 statute (P.L. 106-181). Scores 35.71% (5/14) - the standout finding is Flag5: the COO may earn up to a 30% pay bonus based on an evaluation performed by the same Administrator who oversees and championed the ATO's creation, with no independent verification or public metrics, a textbook self-referential pay-for-performance risk. Combined with unspecified performance criteria (Flag4), concentrated new operational authority (Flag1), and a 5-year-only, non-public reporting cadence (Flag2).

title: Executive Order 13181—To Protect the Privacy of Protected Health Information in Oversight Investigations

flags.justification: Sole authorizing power over law-enforcement use of protected health information discovered during health-oversight activities is vested in the Deputy Attorney General (or DOD General Counsel for military matters), with no independent check on that determination.

The annual report required by Sec.3(d) goes only 'to the President of the United States' - not to Congress or the public - despite otherwise detailed required content (request counts, grant/denial breakdown, requesting agencies, authorized uses).
Not applicable - single coherent subject (health-oversight information privacy).

The authorization standard is a wholly discretionary balancing test with no objective criteria: use is permitted only where 'the public interest and the need for disclosure clearly outweigh the potential for injury to the patient, to the physician-patient relationship, and to the treatment services' (Sec.3(b)).
Not applicable - a privacy-restriction order, no incentive structure at issue.
Not applicable - an ongoing policy restriction, no sunset expected.
No independent-oversight-blocking mechanism; Sec.4's exceptions preserve rather than eliminate pre-existing law-enforcement authorities.
Not applicable - not a third-party grantee/contractor program.

Affects the balance between health-oversight enforcement (e.g., Medicare fraud investigations) and unrelated criminal investigations government-wide, extending the standard previously codified only for administrative-subpoena cases under 18 U.S.C. 3486 to all health-oversight-derived information.
Clean DOJ/DOD coordination (Sec.3(e)), with DOD's parallel report folded into DOJ's consolidated annual report - no conflict.

Sec.4 carves out several significant exceptions: no restriction on derivative use of information obtained in non-health-oversight investigations, no restriction on duties 'imposed by statute,' and no additional limitation on derivative use under 18 U.S.C. 3486 - a genuine, moderately broad exemptions architecture that could narrow the core protection.

notes: Restricts law-enforcement use of HIPAA-related protected health information discovered during health-oversight activities for unrelated investigations, absent Deputy AG/DOD General Counsel authorization under a discretionary balancing test. Scores 35.71% (5/14), driven by concentrated single-official authorizing power (Flag1), a President-only (non-public/non-Congressional) annual report (Flag2), a wholly discretionary vague balancing standard (Flag4), and a genuine multi-part exemptions architecture (Flag11) that could narrow the protection's practical reach.

Not applicable - routine technical/administrative order.

Routine annual Federal pay-schedule adjustment order (General Schedule, Foreign Service, VHA, SES, Executive Schedule, uniformed services, locality payments, administrative law judges) implementing statutory pay-adjustment formulas with no independent discretionary content; supersedes EO13144 of December 21, 1999 (this stream: 0.0%).

title: Executive Order 13183—Establishment of the President's Task Force on Puerto Rico's Status

flags.justification: Broad, evenly distributed Cabinet-wide representation with dual co-chairs (Attorney General's designee and an Interagency Group co-chair) - no single-official concentration.

The order's binding, dated reporting mechanism (Sec.4: report to the President by May 1, 2001, then 'not less than annually') runs to the President alone; Sec.3's reference to providing 'advice and recommendations... to the President and the Congress' is aspirational language describing the Task Force's general function, not a mandatory Congressional-transmittal requirement.
Not applicable - single coherent subject (Puerto Rico status process).
Not applicable - an advisory/consultative task force with no enforcement mechanism.
Not applicable - no incentive structure at issue.
No explicit termination clause for the Task Force itself, notable since the underlying policy question (Puerto Rico's status) is inherently transitional in nature.
Not applicable - no oversight-preemption issue.
Not applicable - not a third-party grant/contract program.

Significant political and constitutional implications for Puerto Rico's status process, including EOP fund-transfer responsibilities to the Commonwealth's Elections Commission for a status referendum (Sec.3), though the Task Force's own role remains deliberative/advisory.
Broad Cabinet-level coordination alongside the pre-existing President's Interagency Group on Puerto Rico, with no evident competing claims.
Not applicable - no exemption categories at issue.

notes: Establishes a Cabinet-designee Task Force implementing P.L. 106-346 to advise on Puerto Rico status options and assist EOP fund-transfer responsibilities for a status-education referendum. Scores 20.0% (2/10), driven by the binding annual report running to the President alone rather than Congress (Flag2) and the Task Force's significant but purely deliberative political second-order effects (Flag9), offset by broad Cabinet-wide (non-concentrated) representation (Flag1 ABSENT).

title: Executive Order 13184—Revocation of Executive Order 12834

flags.justification: A single-sentence order revoking Executive Order 12834 of January 20, 1993 ('Ethics Commitments by Executive Branch Appointees') - Clinton's own first-day ethics-pledge order - effective precisely 'at noon January 20, 2001,' the exact moment his own term ends and his successor is inaugurated.
Not applicable - routine technical/administrative order.
Not applicable - single narrow technical action.
Not applicable - no enforcement mechanism at issue.
Not applicable - no incentive structure created.
Not applicable - not a time-limited grant of authority.

A single-sentence order revoking Executive Order 12834 of January 20, 1993 ('Ethics Commitments by Executive Branch Appointees') - Clinton's own first-day ethics-pledge order - effective precisely 'at noon January 20, 2001,' the exact moment his own term ends and his successor is inaugurated.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive structure.

A structurally minimal but pointedly timed order: by revoking his own administration's ethics-commitment order effective at the literal instant of the presidential transition, it releases Clinton's own appointees and former employees from their revolving-door/lobbying commitments precisely as they leave office - a notable second-order effect that arguably undercuts the deterrent value of the original 1993 pledge, since covered individuals could always know the commitments would lapse at the end of the administration that imposed them.
Not applicable - no competing agency claims.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

notes: A one-sentence revocation of EO12834 (Clinton's own 1993 ethics-pledge order for executive-branch appointees), timed to take effect at noon January 20, 2001 - the precise moment of presidential transition. Scores 16.67% (1/6) via the technical_notable template, reflecting the order's minimal structural content but a genuine, worth-flagging second-order effect (Flag9): releasing an administration's own appointees from binding ethics commitments at the exact instant their terms end.

title: Executive Order 13185—To Strengthen the Federal Government-University Research Partnership

flags.justification: Sets government-wide guiding/operating principles for the Federal-university research partnership without concentrating authority in any single office; OSTP coordinates review but does not direct agency action.

The OSTP review report is delivered 'to the President' only (Sec.2(c)) - no Congressional or public transmittal requirement, despite the report's stated purpose of assessing the 'overall health' of a partnership spanning the entire federal research enterprise.
Not applicable - single coherent subject (federal-university research partnership principles).
Not applicable - a purely aspirational statement of guiding/operating principles with no enforcement mechanism.
Not applicable - no incentive structure at issue.
Not applicable - a permanent framework-setting order, no sunset expected.
No oversight-preemption language.
Not applicable - not a third-party grant/contract program.

The order's principles 'shall provide the framework for the development and analysis of all future Federal policies, rules, and regulations for the Federal Government-University research partnership' (Sec.1(a)) - a broad, government-wide governing framework for the entire federal research-funding ecosystem, though implemented through aspirational principles rather than self-executing rules.
Not applicable - a coordinative framework, not an operational program with competing agency claims.
Not applicable - no exemption categories at issue.

notes: Sets guiding and operating principles (drawn from an April 1999 NSTC report) intended to frame all future federal policy on the Government-University research partnership, with OSTP conducting a periodic review. Scores 25.0% (2/8), driven entirely by the President-only (non-public) review report (Flag2) and the principles' broad prospective governing scope across the entire federal research enterprise (Flag9).

title: Executive Order 13186—Responsibilities of Federal Agencies To Protect Migratory Birds

flags.justification: A broad, multi-agency framework in which the Fish and Wildlife Service coordinates but does not concentrate power - each agency independently negotiates its own MOU.

The Council's annual report of accomplishments (Sec.4(a)(2)) has no specified external recipient in the text, and Sec.5(b) explicitly states the order creates no right or benefit 'enforceable... by a party against the United States' - meaning no citizen or conservation group can sue to enforce agency MOU compliance, a genuine accountability gap.
Not applicable - single coherent subject (migratory bird conservation).

The entire compliance framework rests on voluntary, individually negotiated MOUs with no specified content standards beyond a long aspirational list, is hedged throughout by 'to the extent practicable' and 'as appropriate,' and carries no penalty for an agency's failure to complete its MOU within the 2-year window.
Not applicable - an aspirational conservation directive, no incentive-gaming structure.
Not applicable - a permanent conservation framework, no sunset expected.
No oversight-preemption mechanism.
Not applicable - a federal-agency-only framework, not a third-party grant/contract program.

Directs every federal agency with measurable impact on migratory bird populations to integrate conservation principles into NEPA review, land-management planning, and permitting decisions government-wide - a significant, wide-reaching mandate, though softened by voluntary MOU implementation and 'as practicable' qualifiers.
The interagency Council (Sec.4) is explicitly designed to share information and coordinate, with representation from eight departments/agencies - no evident cannibalization.

Explicitly excludes any action 'delegated to or assumed by nonfederal entities, or carried out by nonfederal entities with Federal assistance' (Sec.2(h)) and any action without a 'measurable negative effect' - broad carve-outs that could allow agencies to avoid coverage for federally-funded but nonfederally-executed activities.

notes: Directs federal agencies to develop MOUs with the Fish and Wildlife Service to conserve migratory bird populations, implementing the Migratory Bird Treaty Act, and establishes an interagency Council for the Conservation of Migratory Birds. Scores 28.57% (4/14), driven by an explicit no-enforceable-rights clause eliminating citizen-suit accountability (Flag2), a heavily hedged and voluntary MOU-based compliance mechanism (Flag4), broad government-wide second-order effects on federal land management and NEPA review (Flag9), and carve-outs excluding federally-assisted-but-nonfederally-executed actions (Flag11).

title: Executive Order 13187—The President's Disability Employment Partnership Board

flags.justification: Establishes a 15-member FACA advisory board (2-year member terms) to advise the President, Vice President, Secretary of Labor and other officials on facilitating employment of people with disabilities; revokes EO12640 (this stream: 16.67%, the prior President's Committee on Employment of People with Disabilities, with employees/records/property/funds transferred to Labor) and amends EO13078 (this stream: 25.0%) to designate the new Board's Chairperson as Vice Chair of the National Task Force on Employment of Adults with Disabilities.

title: Executive Order 13189—Federal Interagency Task Force on the District of Columbia

flags.justification: Chaired and directed by the OMB Director, who is empowered to 'develop, review, modify, and, as appropriate, implement program recommendations' and to unilaterally add 'such other members as the Director... may provide' (Sec.2(b)(18)).

The order contains NO reporting requirement of any kind - no report to the President, Congress, or the public anywhere in its text - formalizing an 18-member Cabinet-level Task Force with real budgetary and programmatic authority over the Nation's Capital with zero mandated transparency mechanism, a stark gap compared to virtually every other task force/commission in this batch.
Not applicable - single coherent subject (District of Columbia federal relations), despite a very broad 18-agency membership.

Responsibilities are described in sweeping, largely undefined terms ('formulate and recommend interagency compacts,' 'provide a forum... and propose and effectuate solutions'), hedged throughout by 'to the extent permitted by law' with no concrete performance standards.
Not applicable - no incentive structure at issue.
No termination clause; formalizes a task force that had already operated informally since 1995 with no expiration in sight.
No explicit oversight-preemption mechanism (distinct from the total-absence-of-reporting finding captured under Flag2).
Not applicable - not a third-party grant/contract program.
Significant effects on DC governance, home-rule dynamics, and economic development, building on the 1997 National Capital Revitalization and Self-Government Improvement Act.
Explicit purpose is to 'better leverage Administration efforts' and coordinate rather than duplicate - no evident cannibalization among the 18 member agencies.
Not applicable - no exemption categories at issue.

notes: Formally establishes the Federal Interagency Task Force on the District of Columbia (18 Cabinet-level members, chaired by the OMB Director) to promote DC's financial stability and self-governance, building on an informal task force operating since 1995. Scores 35.71% (5/14), driven by a CRITICAL finding on Flag2: the order contains no reporting requirement whatsoever - a stark, complete accountability gap for a Cabinet-level body wielding real programmatic authority - compounded by concentrated OMB-Director authority (Flag1) and sweeping, undefined responsibilities (Flag4).
